originalTitle: Chad warns Sudan it will retaliate after drone strike on mourners kills 17
title: News from Our Neighbors: Chad and Sudan
content:
Hello, wonderful friends! Your kid-friendly reporter is here with some important news from our amazing continent, Africa. Today, we're talking about two countries that are neighbors: Chad and Sudan. They share a very long border, just like how you might share a fence with your next-door friend. But sometimes, even friends have big problems. Right now, there's a big problem that needs us to understand it. Let's learn together!

A Sad Day in Chad

Something very sad happened in Chad. Some people were gathered together. They were remembering someone who had passed away. This is called mourning. It's a time when people feel sad and support each other. But during this sad time, something unexpected happened. An object called a "drone" came and caused trouble. This drone hurt many people. Seventeen people lost their lives, which is very, very sad. Imagine if something bad happened while you were at a quiet gathering. Everyone in Chad is very sad about this. It's a big problem when people get hurt.

What is a Drone?

Did you know what a drone is? It's like a small flying robot! Some drones are used for fun, like taking cool pictures from the sky. They can fly high up and help us see things from above. But other drones are bigger and can be used by armies. They can fly without a person inside them. They are controlled by someone far away using a computer. In this sad story, a drone was used in a way that hurt people. It came from the sky and made everyone very scared and sad. It's important to know that drones can be used for good things, like helping farmers or delivering medicines. But sometimes they are used for bad things too, which is very upsetting.

Chad's President Speaks Up

When something this big and sad happens, the leaders of the country need to speak. The leader of Chad is President Idris Mahamat Déby. He is like the head teacher or the captain of the whole country. When he heard the sad news, he was very upset. He said that Chad will not just let this happen. He said that Chad will "retaliate." That's a big word, but it just means they will do something back because they are very sad and angry about what happened. He wants to protect his people. He feels it's his job to keep everyone in Chad safe and make sure such a sad event doesn't happen again.

Closing the Border

President Déby did two very important things right away. First, he ordered a "total closure" of the border. What does that mean? Imagine your house has a door. When you close the door, people can't just walk in or out easily. A border is like a very, very long door between two countries. Chad and Sudan share a border. When President Déby closed it, it means people and cars cannot easily go from Chad to Sudan or from Sudan to Chad. It's like putting a big "STOP" sign there. This is a very serious step because it stops people from visiting or trading between the countries. Did you know? Chad and Sudan share a border that is over 1,400 kilometers long! That's like driving for many, many hours, or walking for many, many days! Closing such a long border is a very serious step. It means the countries are not getting along at all right now. Usually, friends like to visit each other across borders. But when there is trouble, sometimes borders are closed for safety.

The Army on High Alert

The second thing President Déby did was to tell Chad's army to be on "high alert." What does that mean? It means the soldiers in Chad's army must be extra ready. They have to watch very carefully and be prepared for anything. Imagine if there was a big storm coming, and your parents told everyone to be extra careful and ready in the house. That's what "high alert" means for the army. Armies are there to protect the people of a country. They wear uniforms and train a lot. They want to keep everyone safe. When an army is on high alert, it shows that the country's leader is very serious about keeping people safe from any more trouble. They are like very watchful guardians. Did you know? Armies in many African countries, like Chad, also help with other important things, like building roads or helping people during floods. But their main job is to protect the country from danger. Being on "high alert" means they are focused only on protection right now, making sure no more bad things happen.

Chad and Sudan: Neighbors in Africa

Chad and Sudan are big countries right next to each other in Africa. If you look at a map of Africa, you will see them side by side. Chad is to the west, and Sudan is to the east. They are both beautiful countries with many different people and cultures. Like all neighbors, they usually try to be good to each other. They share rivers, and sometimes people travel between them for work or to visit family. It's always best when neighbors live in peace. When countries get along, everyone benefits, and life is much happier for everyone. Did you know? Chad is known for its beautiful desert landscapes and the amazing Lake Chad! Lake Chad is a very important source of water for many people and animals. Sudan has the famous River Nile flowing through it, which is one of the longest rivers in the world! Both countries are full of rich history and wonderful natural beauty. It's sad when problems happen between such special places.

Why Peace is Important

It is always important for countries to live in peace. When countries fight, many people get hurt, and life becomes very difficult. Children might not be able to go to school, and families might have to leave their homes. That's why leaders always try to find ways to talk and solve problems without fighting. Talking helps us understand each other and find solutions. Did you know? There are special groups in the world, like the African Union and the United Nations, that try to help countries be friends and solve their problems peacefully. They send people to talk to leaders and help them find good solutions so everyone can be safe and happy. Everyone hopes Chad and Sudan can find a peaceful way to talk about this very sad event.

What Happens Next?

For now, Chad's border is closed, and its army is ready. Everyone hopes that leaders from both Chad and Sudan can talk to each other very soon. We hope they can find a way to stop any more sadness and bring peace back. It's important to remember that even when big problems happen, people can often find ways to talk and make things better. We send our thoughts to everyone in Chad and hope for peace to return quickly. Your friendly reporter will keep you updated!
summary: Chad's President is very upset and has closed the border with Sudan and made the army ready after a drone from Sudan hurt many people in Chad, and everyone hopes they can talk and find peace.
whyMatters: This is important because when countries have problems, people can get hurt, and it stops friends and families from visiting each other, so we hope they can talk and make peace again.

originalTitle: Denounce 'abject' Afcon decision - senior Caf member
title: Denounce 'abject' Afcon decision - senior Caf member
content: Caf executive committee member Augustin Senghor says the decision to strip Senegal of the 2025 Afcon title is "unacceptable, abject and we have to denounce it".

originalTitle: UK agrees deal to ease migrant returns to Nigeria
title: Big News! Nigeria and UK Are Super Friends!
content:
Hello amazing kids! It's your friendly news reporter for Africa here, with some super exciting news! Today, we are going to talk about something very special that happened with our dear country, Nigeria.

Our Amazing President Went on a Big Trip!

Guess what? Our President, the number one leader of Nigeria, went on a big trip! He flew far, far away to a country called the United Kingdom, or the UK for short. Imagine going on an airplane to a brand new place! It must have been very exciting. Our President is a very important person. He helps to make sure our country is safe and happy. He makes big decisions for all of us. And he also talks to other leaders around the world.

A Very Special Visit: A State Visit!

This was not just any trip. It was a super-duper special trip called a 'State Visit'! It's like when you invite a very, very important guest to your home. You make sure everything is perfect, and you talk about important things. That's what a State Visit is all about. The King of the UK and other important people there invited our President. They wanted to talk and become even better friends. Countries talk to each other to help solve problems and make life better for everyone.

Did you know?

Nigeria is a very big country in Africa, full of wonderful people and places! The UK is an island country, meaning it has water all around it, and it's quite far from us!

First Time in a Super Long Time!

This visit was extra, extra special because it was the first time a leader from West Africa (that's where Nigeria is!) has gone on a State Visit to the UK in a very, very long time. How long? Try 37 years! Can you imagine 37 years? That's more years than many of your parents have been alive! It's like waiting for 37 birthdays to come and go. That's a super long time! So, this visit was a really big deal for Nigeria and for all of West Africa. Our President getting this special invitation shows that Nigeria is a very important country in the world. Other countries want to be friends with us and work with us.

What Did They Talk About?

When our President and the UK leaders met, they talked about many important things. Just like you talk with your friends about your games and plans, leaders talk about big plans for their countries. One big thing they talked about was helping people. They wanted to make things easier and safer for people who travel between Nigeria and the UK.

Making a Deal to Help People

They made a special promise, an 'agreement', or a 'deal', as we call it in the news. This deal is all about helping people who have traveled from Nigeria to the UK. Sometimes, people move from their home country to another country. They might go to find new jobs, or to study in new schools, or to be with their family. These people are sometimes called 'migrants'. They are just people looking for a new place to live for a while.

Did you know?

Moving to a new place can be an adventure! But sometimes, people need help to go back to their first home.

Helping People Come Back Home Safely

This deal is about helping those people who might need to come back to Nigeria. Maybe they went to the UK, but they don't have the right papers to stay there for a long, long time. Or maybe they just miss their home and family in Nigeria! The UK and Nigeria want to make sure that if someone needs to come back home to Nigeria, they can do so in a very safe and easy way. No one wants to be lost or feel unsafe when they are traveling. This deal means that Nigeria and the UK will work together like super good friends. They will help make sure that when people return, everything goes smoothly. They will make sure people are treated well and can get back to their families in Nigeria without any big problems.

Why is This Deal Good for Us?

This agreement is very good news for many reasons! 1. **Safety First!** It helps to keep our people safe. Everyone deserves to travel safely and come home safely. 2. **Super Friends!** It shows that Nigeria and the UK are strong friends. When countries are friends, they can help each other with many things, like building better schools, improving health, and even trading cool things. 3. **Working Together!** It means our countries can work together to solve problems. This makes the world a better place for everyone. 4. **Fair Rules!** It makes sure that everyone follows the rules. This keeps things fair for all people who want to travel or live in another country.

Did you know?

When countries are friends, they can share ideas and learn from each other! It's like when you play with your friends and learn new games. So, our President's trip was a very, very important one. It helped Nigeria make even stronger friends with the UK. And it's all about making sure that our people are safe and happy, wherever they are in the world. What a great day for Nigeria and for friendship between countries!
summary: Nigeria's President visited the UK for a special 'State Visit', the first by a West African leader in 37 years, where they made a deal to help people return safely to Nigeria from the UK.
whyMatters: This is important because it shows Nigeria is a strong country with friends around the world, and it means our leaders are working to keep our people safe and happy, even when they travel far from home.

originalTitle: Uefa calls leagues to summit over 'microscopic' VAR
title: Super Soccer Eyes Get a Check-Up!
content:
Hello, amazing African football fans! It's your kid-friendly news reporter here, bringing you some super exciting news from the world of football! Even though this story is about teams far away in Europe, it's about something that makes football fair and fun for everyone, everywhere – and that includes us, right here in Africa!

Football: The Beautiful Game!

Do you love football? I know I do! It's that wonderful game where two teams try to kick a round ball into a net called a goal. The team that scores more goals wins! Millions and millions of people all over the world love to play and watch football. It's so exciting! We see amazing passes, super speedy dribbles, and powerful shots!

Who Helps Keep the Game Fair?

In every football game, there's a very important person called the referee. The referee wears a special uniform and runs around the field with the players. Their job is to make sure everyone follows the rules. They blow a whistle if there's a mistake, or a "foul." They decide if a goal counts or if a player needs to get a yellow or red card. It's a very big job with lots of decisions to make, super quickly! **Did you know?** In Africa, football is one of the most loved sports! Kids play it everywhere – in schools, on dusty fields, and even just on the street with their friends!

Meet VAR: Football's Super Helper Camera!

Sometimes, even the best referees can miss something because the game is so fast! Imagine if a player was *just* offside before scoring a goal, or if a foul happened *just* inside the penalty box. These tiny details are hard to see with just your eyes! That's why, a few years ago, something super cool was invented to help referees. It's called VAR! VAR stands for **V**ideo **A**ssistant **R**eferee. Think of it like a team of special helper detectives who watch the game on many, many TV screens. They have lots of cameras showing the game from every single angle you can imagine! These helpers are in a special room, watching everything super carefully.

How Does VAR Work?

When something really important happens in a game, like a goal being scored, a big foul, or a red card decision, the VAR team quickly reviews it. They watch replays in slow motion, from different cameras. If they see a clear mistake that the referee on the field made, they talk to the referee through a special earpiece. The referee on the field can then listen to what the VAR team says. Sometimes, the referee will even run to the side of the field to a special TV screen called the "Referee Review Area" (RRA). There, they can watch the replay themselves and make the final decision. This helps make sure the *really big* decisions are always correct and fair! **Did you know?** VAR was first used in football matches in the Netherlands, a country far away in Europe, before it became popular around the world!

Why Are People Talking About VAR Now?

Even though VAR is a great helper, sometimes people think it's not perfect yet. Imagine if you have a new toy. At first, it's super cool, but maybe you find a tiny little thing you'd like to change to make it even better, right? Well, that's a bit like what's happening with VAR. Some people, like the big football clubs and the people who run the leagues (where teams play games every week), feel that VAR is looking at things *too* closely. They call these "microscopic" decisions. This means VAR is sometimes looking at tiny, tiny details that are almost impossible to see, and it can take a long, long time to decide.

What is the Big Meeting About?

So, the "big boss" group for football in Europe, called UEFA (you can say "YOU-eff-ah"), has invited all the leaders from the different football leagues to a special meeting. Imagine all the head teachers from different schools coming together to talk about how to make playtime better! That's kind of what's happening. They want to sit down and have a big chat about how VAR is being used. They want to make sure it's helping the game, not slowing it down too much, or confusing people. They want to find ways to make VAR even better and faster, so that the game keeps flowing and everyone understands the decisions. **Did you know?** The World Cup, which is the biggest football tournament in the world, also uses VAR to help make decisions fair! Many African teams play in the World Cup!

Making Football Fairer and Faster!

The leaders will talk about things like: * How long should VAR decisions take? Can we make them quicker? * Are we using VAR for the right things, or looking at details that are too small? * How can we explain VAR decisions better to the fans watching? They want to hear ideas from everyone involved – the clubs, the players, and the fans too! They all want the same thing: fair football that is fun to watch and easy to understand.

Why is This Important for Us?

Even though this meeting is happening far away in Europe, it's important for us, the young football fans in Africa, too! Why? Because football is a global game! What happens in one part of the world can affect how football is played and enjoyed everywhere. If VAR gets better and makes football even fairer and more exciting, then that's good news for all of us who love to watch our favourite teams play, whether it's in a big stadium or on a small TV screen at home. We want justice for our teams, right? So, let's cheer on the football leaders as they have their big chat about VAR. Let's hope they find the best ways to make football even more amazing for all of us! Keep loving football, keep playing fair, and keep supporting your favourite teams! See you next time, amazing kids!
summary: Big football leaders in Europe are having a meeting to talk about how the helper cameras, called VAR, are working in games and how to make them even better.
whyMatters: This is important because we all love fair football, and they want to make sure the helper cameras make the game even better and more fun for everyone, especially for us watching!

originalTitle: English dominance of Champions League group phase prompts debate
title: English dominance of Champions League group phase prompts debate
content: A number of European clubs call for a change to the Champions League rules to allow teams from the same country to draw each other in the league phase.
